title: Regex patterns following a possessive quantifier should not always fail
description: Avoid using possessive quantifiers ('+?', '??', etc.) at the end of a regex pattern, as they can cause unexpected behavior when the pattern should match zero or more occurrences of the preceding element.
message: Possessive quantifier found at the end of a regex pattern
recommendation: Rewrite the regex pattern to avoid using possessive quantifiers at the end

noncompliant.code:
import re
pattern = r'(.*?)+?'
matches = re.findall(pattern, 'abc')
assert len(matches) > 0
noncompliant.description: Using a possessive quantifier at the end of a regex pattern

compliant.code:
import re
pattern = r'(.*?)*
matches = re.findall(pattern, 'abc')
assert len(matches) > 0
compliant.description: Rewritten regex pattern without a possessive quantifier at the end

why_is_this_an_issue: Possessive quantifiers can cause unexpected behavior when the pattern should match zero or more occurrences of the preceding element.
how_to_fix: Rewrite the regex pattern to avoid using possessive quantifiers at the end

metadata.title: Regex patterns following a possessive quantifier should not always fail
